EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strengthening low level jet in response to an approaching system produced a minor mountain wave event, primarily in the wind prone Smokies.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Park Service weather station recorded a 67 mph non-thunderstorm gust at Cove Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A higher elevation snowfall occurred during the evening of the 14th into the morning of the 15th thanks to a passing upper trough and associated front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two bouts of snow fell in the Smoky Mountains, with 7 inches of snow observed at the top of Mount LeConte at the lodge (Elevation 6,400 feet), and then 2.1 inches of snow observed by the automated station at Newfound Gap (Elevation 5,049 feet).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level weather system produced a light snow event in the mountains of East Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two inches of snow was observed at the lodge on Mount LeConte and at the Newfound Gap automated station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level weather system produced a light snow event in the mountains of East Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two inches of snow fell at Roan Mountain State Park (2800 feet elevation) and two inches of snow fell at 3500 feet of elevation in Roan Mountain village.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant snowstorm struck portions of East Tennessee at the end of the month, thanks to strong lift from a deep upper trough diving from north to south. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two inches of snow fell in Oneida.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry, mid-level shortwave aid in the push of another cold front in late January. Another arctic airmass residing behind this boundary resulting in another bitterly cold period. Cold temperatures combined with elevated northerly winds resulted in sub zero wind chill values across West Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum wind chill of 1F recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light snow developed over eastern Middle Tennessee during the afternoon of January 15, 2026. While snow accumulations were less than one half inch, a quick burst of snow resulted in slick road conditions near Cookeville in Putnam County. Slick roads and poor visibility resulted in a multi-car crash on Interstate 40.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A quick burst of snow resulted in slick roads and poor visibility on Interstate 40 near Cookeville. Near mile marker 285, these poor conditions caused an eleven chain-reaction crash that forced the closure of the Interstate for several hours. Three injuries were reported in the accident. Damages are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice storm crippled areas from northern Mississippi through middle Tennessee, clipping far northwest Alabama from the 24th through the 27th. Freezing rain accumulated up to 1 inch in far northwest Alabama, with accumulations dropping off quickly going further east and southeast in north Alabama and southern middle Tennessee. The initial onset of precipitation was a mixture of snow, sleet and freezing rain, changing to all freezing rain in far northwest Alabama. A surge of warm air on the 26th changed precipitation to moderate to heavy rain for much of north Alabama and southern middle Tennessee, as temperatures warmed well above freezing. The exception was the far northwest tip of Alabama which remained in the colder air. A reinforcing cold front eventually brought temperatures back below freezing quickly during the late afternoon and early evening hours of the 25th which ended the steady rainfall. However, areas of light freezing drizzle redeveloped and produced a light accumulation of glaze across much of the area, and existing water on roadways from earlier heavy rain also began to freeze. The freezing drizzle changed to light snow and flurries during the overnight, lasting into the morning hours of the 26th. Very light accumulations were reported, with a dusting up to one half inch. Sunshine melted  the snow quickly during the day.||Impacts from these events were most severe in far northwest Alabama, including devestating tree damage, including numerous trees and limbs being knocked down, split or toppled onto power lines. Power outages occurred in northwest Alabama, including the Quad Cities/Shoals metro area. A water treatment facility in parts of western Lauderdale County was damaged, and a boil order was declared for about 1500 residents affected. Schools and businesses were shut down for multiple days in northwest Alabama and southern middle Tennessee. In north central and northeast Alabama, impacts were much less severe, and schools and businesses were shut down on the 26th due to cold and icy spots on roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas of freezing drizzle moved in during the late afternoon and evening of the 25th. This precipitation changed to light snow and flurries during the overnight hours and ended during the late morning of the 26th. An additional light glaze of ice and fluffy snow accumulation of an inch or less was reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill temperatures dropped as low as 1 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Below normal precipitation amounts continued through much of January 2026. The largest deficits of 2 to 3 inches were found across northwest Middle Tennessee which saw severe drought conditions develop across portions of Stewart, Houston, and Montgomery counties. A major winter storm with substantial liquid precipitation brought slight improvement to Middle Tennessee during the end of the month. By the end of January, 30 percent of the area was in at least moderate drought conditions with 6 percent of this classified as severe drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions across eastern Cumberland County from December 2025 continued through January 2026.

Freezing rain accumulated up to 1 inch in far northwest Alabama, with accumulations dropping off quickly going further east and southeast in north Alabama and southern middle Tennessee. The initial onset of precipitation was a mixture of snow, sleet and freezing rain, changing to all freezing rain in far northwest Alabama. A surge of warm air on the 26th changed precipitation to moderate to heavy rain for much of north Alabama and southern middle Tennessee, as temperatures warmed well above freezing. The exception was the far northwest tip of Alabama which remained in the colder air. A reinforcing cold front eventually brought temperatures back below freezing quickly during the late afternoon and early evening hours of the 25th which ended the steady rainfall. However, areas of light freezing drizzle redeveloped and produced a light accumulation of glaze across much of the area, and existing water on roadways from earlier heavy rain also began to freeze. The freezing drizzle changed to light snow and flurries during the overnight, lasting into the morning hours of the 26th. Very light accumulations were reported, with a dusting up to one half inch. Sunshine melted  the snow quickly during the day.||Impacts from these events were most severe in far northwest Alabama, including devestating tree damage, including numerous trees and limbs being knocked down, split or toppled onto power lines. Power outages occurred in northwest Alabama, including the Quad Cities/Shoals metro area. A water treatment facility in parts of western Lauderdale County was damaged, and a boil order was declared for about 1500 residents affected. Schools and businesses were shut down for multiple days in northwest Alabama and southern middle Tennessee. In north central and northeast Alabama, impacts were much less severe, and schools and businesses were shut down on the 26th due to cold and icy spots on roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intermittent sleet changed to freezing rain during the morning hours. A light glaze of ice was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm system impacted the State of Tennessee starting late Friday, January 23 and continuing into Sunday, January 25, 2026. Cold air moved into Tennessee Friday evening, setting the stage for winter weather through the weekend. This cold air along with a potent storm system resulted in primarily snow Friday night into Saturday morning. However, as warm air aloft moved north into Tennessee, this changed the precipitation from snow to sleet and then finally to freezing rain Saturday evening through Sunday afternoon. By the time precipitation came to an end, snow accumulations were highest across northwest Middle Tennessee where generally 3 to 5 inches were measured. A swath of ice accumulations of 0.25 to 0.50 inches was reported from Wayne County northeast through Nashville and to the Tennessee/Kentucky border. Locally higher amounts over one half inch were reported around the Nashville metro area.||These accumulations caused widespread, crippling damage in the form of broken and downed trees and power lines, dangerous to impossible travel conditions, and hundreds of thousands of power outages. Nashville Electric Service (NES) alone reported 230,000 customers without power which is the largest number of outages at one time NES has ever experienced. ||The other major impact from this winter storm was frigid temperatures. Cold air already in place over the weekend was reinforced Sunday night as arctic air moved into Tennessee behind this winter storm. The coldest temperature readings were recorded Monday and Tuesday mornings with widespread readings in the single digits and wind chills below zero. With these very cold temperatures, impacts were felt long after the precipitation ended.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mixture of snow, sleet, and ice impacted Hickman County during the early morning hours of January 24th into the afternoon of January 25th. A variety of reporting sources measured snow accumulations of 1 to 2 inches with ice accumulations of 0.20 to 0.30 inches. This led to several reports of treacherous travel and downed powerlines, trees, and tree branches. Total power outages were 4,636 customers across Hickman County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm system impacted the State of Tennessee starting late Friday, January 23 and continuing into Sunday, January 25, 2026. Cold air moved into Tennessee Friday evening, setting the stage for winter weather through the weekend. This cold air along with a potent storm system resulted in primarily snow Friday night into Saturday morning. However, as warm air aloft moved north into Tennessee, this changed the precipitation from snow to sleet and then finally to freezing rain Saturday evening through Sunday afternoon. By the time precipitation came to an end, snow accumulations were highest across northwest Middle Tennessee where generally 3 to 5 inches were measured. A swath of ice accumulations of 0.25 to 0.50 inches was reported from Wayne County northeast through Nashville and to the Tennessee/Kentucky border. Cold air already in place over the weekend was reinforced Sunday night as arctic air moved into Tennessee behind this winter storm. The coldest temperature readings were recorded Monday and Tuesday mornings with widespread readings in the single digits and wind chills below zero. With these very cold temperatures, impacts were felt long after the precipitation ended.

This led to several reports of treacherous travel and downed powerlines, trees, and tree branches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Broad scale upper lift led to a complex winter storm across East Tennessee, with onset precipitation of snow transitioning to freezing rain before ending as rain as the temperatures rose through the evening, night, and following morning. This storm led to 7 fatalities in East Tennessee. On the morning of the 25th the strengthening low level jet that was advancing warm air northwards led to a high end mountain wave wind event in portions of the East Tennessee mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two bouts of wintry weather occurred in Sullivan, with snow and sleet transitioned to a long duration freezing rain event before transitioning to rain and then ending with light snow. Ice accumulations of 0.25 to 0.35 inches of ice were observed, with an isolated report of a half inch of ice. Additionally, a half inch of snow and sleet were observed, the Tri-Cities Airport measured 0.6 inches of snow and sleet.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two bouts of wintry weather with ice accretion of 0.4 inches measured in Jonesborough. Snow and sleet amounts between the two rounds of wintry weather resulted in 0.8 inches of total accumulation in Washington County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two bouts of winter precipitation led to heavy freezing rain accumulation and light snow in the first bout, with light snow in the second bout. Ice accumulations ranging from 0.25 to 0.50 inch were reported by both CoCoRaHS observations in Mooresburg and Church Hill along with 2 inches of snow in Church Hill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: January featured multiple precipitation events, including rainfall and a significant winter storm (snow/ice) late in the month, which affected much of the Mid-South. These events contributed to short-term surface moisture gains. Despite these events, precipitation was not sufficient or consistent enough to fully eliminate long-term deficits, especially in areas that experienced prolonged dryness during fall 2025.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: D2 Severe Drought conditions emerged in mid-to-late January and continued into February.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While the wintry precipitation created the initial impacts, it was the multi-day period of subfreezing temperatures that prolonged and, in many cases, worsened those impacts. The lack of meaningful thawing effectively extended the storm���s footprint from a 2���3 day event into a nearly week-long hazard across West Tennessee. Limited melting during the day and refreezing each night kept roads hazardous well beyond the end of precipitation, especially on secondary roads and bridges. Prolonged freezing temperatures increased the risk of burst pipes, particularly in homes and buildings without adequate insulation or during extended power outages. The duration of cold posed risks for hypothermia and exposure, especially for vulnerable populations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum wind chill of -2F reported.

Limited melting during the day and refreezing each night kept roads hazardous well beyond the end of precipitation, especially on secondary roads and bridges. Prolonged freezing temperatures increased the risk of burst pipes, particularly in homes and buildings without adequate insulation or during extended power outages. The duration of cold posed risks for hypothermia and exposure, especially for vulnerable populations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum windchill of -2F recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant snowstorm struck portions of East Tennessee at the end of the month, thanks to strong lift from a deep upper trough diving from north to south. A couple of long term cooperative weather stations saw top 10 snowfalls during this storm. This storm led to 3 confirmed fatalities in East Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across Sullivan County, with 8.5 inches of snow in Gray and 9 inches in Colonial Heights. Additional snowfall measurements include 7.5 inches in Bristol, and the Tri-Cities Airport measured 8 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant snowstorm struck portions of East Tennessee at the end of the month, thanks to strong lift from a deep upper trough diving from north to south. A couple of long term cooperative weather stations saw top 10 snowfalls during this storm. This storm led to 3 confirmed fatalities in East Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A significant snow fell across Greene County, with 12.5 inches of snow reported in Greeneville. Additional observations include 9 inches in Mosheim, 7.9 inches in Midway, 10.5 inches on the south side of Greeneville, and 12 inches closer to the Smokies. The cooperative observer reported 12.8 inches, which would be the 8th highest two-day snowstorm in the station's 135 year history.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Broad scale upper lift led to a complex winter storm across East Tennessee, with onset precipitation of snow transitioning to freezing rain before ending as rain as the temperatures rose through the evening, night, and following morning. This storm led to 7 fatalities in East Tennessee. On the morning of the 25th the strengthening low level jet that was advancing warm air northwards led to a high end mountain wave wind event in portions of the East Tennessee mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two inches of snow and an unknown amount of freezing rain fell in Scott County during the two distinct periods of precipitation, with wintry mix in the first bout, then rain, then snow to end.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While the wintry precipitation created the initial impacts, it was the multi-day period of subfreezing temperatures that prolonged and, in many cases, worsened those impacts. The lack of meaningful thawing effectively extended the storm���s footprint from a 2���3 day event into a nearly week-long hazard across West Tennessee. Limited melting during the day and refreezing each night kept roads hazardous well beyond the end of precipitation, especially on secondary roads and bridges. Prolonged freezing temperatures increased the risk of burst pipes, particularly in homes and buildings without adequate insulation or during extended power outages. The duration of cold posed risks for hypothermia and exposure, especially for vulnerable populations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum wind chill of -5F recorded.

Limited melting during the day and refreezing each night kept roads hazardous well beyond the end of precipitation, especially on secondary roads and bridges. Prolonged freezing temperatures increased the risk of burst pipes, particularly in homes and buildings without adequate insulation or during extended power outages. The duration of cold posed risks for hypothermia and exposure, especially for vulnerable populations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum wind chill of -3F reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Broad scale upper lift led to a complex winter storm across East Tennessee, with onset precipitation of snow transitioning to freezing rain before ending as rain as the temperatures rose through the evening, night, and following morning. This storm led to 7 fatalities in East Tennessee. On the morning of the 25th the strengthening low level jet that was advancing warm air northwards led to a high end mountain wave wind event in portions of the East Tennessee mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wintry mix then rain then snow fell in Morgan County through the duration of the event. Public report of 1.5 inches of snow in Pine Orchard.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A long duration and high end mountain wave wind event occurred in the Sevier Smokies, with a 93 mph peak gust measured at 11 AM at Cove Mountain in the national park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: January featured multiple precipitation events, including rainfall and a significant winter storm (snow/ice) late in the month, which affected much of the Mid-South. These events contributed to short-term surface moisture gains. Despite these events, precipitation was not sufficient or consistent enough to fully eliminate long-term deficits, especially in areas that experienced prolonged dryness during fall 2025.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: D2 Severe Drought conditions emerged in mid-to-late January and continued into February.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant snowstorm struck portions of East Tennessee at the end of the month, thanks to strong lift from a deep upper trough diving from north to south. A couple of long term cooperative weather stations saw top 10 snowfalls during this storm. This storm led to 3 confirmed fatalities in East Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch of snow was measured near Oliver Springs. Social media reports have widespread estimated snowfall near or over an inch in Wartburg and Lancing. Two inches of snow estimated from social media report in Wartburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Broad scale upper lift led to a complex winter storm across East Tennessee, with onset precipitation of snow transitioning to freezing rain before ending as rain as the temperatures rose through the evening, night, and following morning. This storm led to 7 fatalities in East Tennessee. On the morning of the 25th the strengthening low level jet that was advancing warm air northwards led to a high end mountain wave wind event in portions of the East Tennessee mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow between 1 and 8 pm LST during the first bout yielded an inch of snowfall north of Bean Station before freezing rain led to at least a glaze of ice accumulation on elevated surfaces. A CoCoRaHS report near Sneedville measured event total snowfall of 1.4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Broad scale upper lift led to a complex winter storm across East Tennessee, with onset precipitation of snow transitioning to freezing rain before ending as rain as the temperatures rose through the evening, night, and following morning. This storm led to 7 fatalities in East Tennessee. On the morning of the 25th the strengthening low level jet that was advancing warm air northwards led to a high end mountain wave wind event in portions of the East Tennessee mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two bouts of wintry weather occurred in Hamblen County as observed at the NWS office in Morristown. First bout led to 0.3 inches of snow and sleet followed by a transition to freezing rain that resulted in 0.2 inches of ice accumulation. Rain then followed before 11 PM LST and persisted for much of the 25th. Backside light snow led to another 0.3 inches of accumulation before ending.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mountain wave wind event results in a measured gust of 61 mph at the NOAA ATDD weather station in Camp Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant snowstorm struck portions of East Tennessee at the end of the month, thanks to strong lift from a deep upper trough diving from north to south. A couple of long term cooperative weather stations saw top 10 snowfalls during this storm. This storm led to 3 confirmed fatalities in East Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant snowfall fell in Gatlinburg and up on the mountains. The Cooperative station in Gatlinburg recorded 15 inches of snow, which ranks 6th place overall in its 125 year record for two-day snowstorms. Additional public reports of 10 and 11 inches of snow were also received from Gatlinburg. Mount LeConte recorded an 11 inch snow depth during the storm, while Newfound Gap reported 5 inches of fresh snow.
